title: Chunking strategies for enterprise RAG
content: Fixed-size chunking is a starting point, not a strategy. Production RAG pipelines benefit from semantic chunking with overlap, parent-document retrieval, and contextual headers prepended at index time.

title: Why naive RAG fails at enterprise scale
content: Naive top-k retrieval breaks down when the corpus has duplicate, near-duplicate, and stale passages. Re-ranking and deduplication move from optional to mandatory above ten million chunks.

title: Multi-tenant RAG with per-customer indices
content: Hard-isolating tenants by index gives the cleanest blast-radius story for compliance auditors and the simplest mental model for capacity planning. Aliases let a single query hit a tenant subset.

title: Recursive RAG: when one retrieval pass is not enough
content: Multi-hop questions require multi-hop retrieval. Generate sub-queries from the original question, fan out, and merge with a reciprocal-rank-fusion or learned re-ranker.

title: Citation-first RAG for regulated industries
content: When auditors ask which document a recommendation came from, the answer cannot be reconstructed from a vector. Persist source URLs, chunk IDs, and retrieval scores alongside every generated answer.

title: HNSW vs IVF vs DiskANN: choosing the right index
content: HNSW dominates for sub-second p95 on under 100M vectors. IVF wins when memory is constrained. DiskANN matters above one billion vectors when SSD becomes the only place that fits.

title: Scalar quantization: 4x memory savings for free
content: SQ8 quantization compresses each dimension from float32 to uint8. Recall drops by less than half a point on most embedding models. SQ4 doubles the savings for noisier corpora.

title: Why product quantization is making a comeback
content: PQ fell out of fashion when IVFSQ8 became the default, but with billion-scale vector workloads on commodity hardware, PQ's 16-32x compression is the only thing that fits.

title: Cosine vs dot product vs L2: similarity metrics that matter
content: OpenAI and Voyage embeddings are L2-normalized, so cosine and dot product give identical rankings. Use dot product to skip a normalization pass per query.

title: HNSW parameter tuning: M, ef_construction, ef_search
content: M=16 and ef_construction=200 are safe defaults. Raising ef_search at query time trades latency for recall without rebuilding the graph.

title: Why pure vector search underperforms on names and SKUs
content: Embedding models smear exact identifiers across dense space. A search for SKU-12345 should match the literal token, not its semantic neighborhood. Hybrid search fixes this.

title: Reciprocal rank fusion: the simplest hybrid scoring
content: RRF takes ordinal rank from BM25 and ordinal rank from kNN, sums the reciprocals, and re-ranks. No score normalization, no learned weights, predictable behavior.

title: Single-pass hybrid: scoring lexical and vector together
content: When the engine fuses BM25 and HNSW scores in one query plan, you skip the round-trip cost of two independent searches and the merge becomes O(k) instead of O(n).

title: Boosting strategies for hybrid relevance
content: Multiply BM25 by 1.0 and kNN by 0.8 as a starting boost ratio. Tune on a held-out evaluation set. Never tune on the corpus you trained the embedding model on.

title: Filtered hybrid search: post-filter vs pre-filter
content: Post-filtering kills recall when filters are selective. Pre-filtering during HNSW traversal is the only correct answer at production scale, but requires the index to support it.

title: p95 latency budgets for interactive RAG agents
content: If the LLM call is 800ms, retrieval has 200ms before perceived latency tanks. Anything over a second on retrieval makes the agent feel broken regardless of answer quality.

title: Cold start latency in vector databases
content: Restart latency matters more than steady-state throughput when you autoscale. A search engine that takes 15 seconds to warm cannot be scaled in under a minute.

title: Observability for retrieval pipelines
content: Log every query, the top-k ids returned, the score distribution, and the chunk source. Without retrieval traces you cannot tell whether a hallucination is a model bug or a context bug.

title: Tail latency under concurrent load
content: Median latency under load is a vanity metric. p99 is what your customers feel. Measure under k-times-expected concurrency, not under benchmark-friendly serial queries.

title: Memory floor: the silent capacity planning trap
content: An idle search node holding a billion vectors should not cost 64GB of resident memory. JVM-based engines treat memory as a single pool; native engines free what they are not using.

title: Data residency for LLM-grounded retrieval
content: EU data must not cross into US-hosted vector databases without an SCC. The embedding is derived data; regulators treat it as personal data when reidentification is feasible.

title: PII in embeddings: the inversion attack
content: Recent research shows embeddings can be partially inverted to recover the source text. Treat embedding stores like databases of personal data, not opaque vectors.

title: Audit logging for AI retrieval
content: Every retrieval that grounds a customer-facing answer must be reproducible six months later. Store query, retrieved IDs, and snapshot version. Append-only, signed, retained per policy.

title: SOC 2 controls that apply to vector workloads
content: Access control, change management, encryption at rest, encryption in transit, and incident response apply to retrieval infrastructure exactly the same way they apply to your primary database.

title: Tenant isolation under shared-infrastructure RAG
content: Cross-tenant retrieval leakage is the highest-severity bug class in multi-tenant RAG. Filter at the index level, not in the application layer, and verify with chaos tests.

title: Why managed vector DBs get expensive at scale
content: Per-vector pricing models look attractive at the proof-of-concept stage and become unsupportable at production scale. At a billion vectors, managed-DB bills routinely exceed the team that built the product.

title: Calculating TCO: storage, search, vector together
content: Run the math on the unified cost: hot search cluster, cold log archive, vector database, embedding API. Fragmented stacks compound cost; a single store collapses three line items into one.

title: Embedding cost is a fixed cost, search cost is variable
content: You pay to embed once. You pay to search forever. Optimize search-side first; the embedding bill amortizes within a quarter, the search bill is the rest of your career.

title: JVM heap is a hidden tax on retrieval workloads
content: Half your search cluster's memory budget goes to GC headroom you will never use. Native engines reclaim that overhead; the cost difference shows up as headcount, not just hardware.

title: Disk efficiency for billion-document corpora
content: Modern compression (Zstd-19 at the segment level) gives 3-4x reductions on log and document data without measurable query-time penalty. SSD lasts twice as long; backup windows halve.

title: Migrating from Elasticsearch: what breaks and what works
content: Standard query DSL works unchanged in 98% of cases. The 2% that breaks tends to be x-pack features, painless scripts, and cluster-shape assumptions. Plan for a four-week test phase, not a weekend cutover.

title: Pinecone exit strategy for cost-pressured teams
content: Export vectors via the Pinecone API, ingest into a self-hosted index, run shadow traffic for two weeks, compare recall@k and latency. The annual savings are typically larger than the migration cost.

title: Decommissioning a Splunk SIEM: the realistic path
content: Move new sources to the new store first; let the Splunk index age out per retention policy. A six-month dual-write period costs less than the all-at-once cutover and removes the rollback risk.

title: Drop-in compatibility: the 80/20 of search migration
content: If your client libraries point at port 9200 and speak ES query DSL, eighty percent of the migration is done. The remaining twenty percent is x-pack-shaped and worth scoping carefully up front.

title: Dual-write patterns for zero-downtime cutover
content: Write to both old and new stores during the migration window. Read from old, validate against new asynchronously. Cut over reads only when the diff between systems is steadily zero.

title: What is agent memory, really?
content: Agent memory is retrieval over a session, a user, and an organization, on three different time horizons. Treat it as three indices, not one, with three different retention policies.

title: Long-context windows do not replace memory
content: A million-token context is still a context, not memory. Memory persists across sessions. Context resets every turn. Build retrieval from memory into the context; do not assume context is durable.

title: Per-user memory namespaces and the right to be forgotten
content: Memory tied to a user must be deletable on request within the regulator's deadline. Index per user, not per agent, so deletion is a single operation rather than a fleet-wide sweep.

title: Episodic vs semantic memory for agents
content: Episodic memory is the transcript of what happened. Semantic memory is the distilled fact. Production agents need both, indexed differently, retrieved by different queries, retained for different durations.

title: Memory consolidation: when to summarize, when to keep raw
content: Raw episodic memory grows unboundedly. Summarize after 30 days; keep summaries forever. Anchor summaries to the original chunks with foreign keys for the regulator who eventually asks.
